unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Bring together your strongest vocabulary, reading, grammar, speaking and writing from Units 1-9. Create a researched multi-section English product for a defined audience, defend it clearly, respond to questions and reflect on your Grade 6 growth.
unit.learningPath: Choose and annotate evidence. Form a research question. Compare sources. Plan and draft sections. Improve from feedback. Present and defend. Reflect. Complete the final course quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 6 learner defending a researched English capstone

readings.title: Review text 1: There's a Lesson in That: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Read the following fable carefully. As you read, think about what lesson the story is trying to teach. Pay close attention to the characters, the problem they face, and how the problem is resolved. Notice how the characters change and what they learn along the way. After reading, you will answer questions about the text at three different levels of difficulty.
In a small village near the coast of East Africa, there lived a woman named Nadia who was known across the region for her extraordinary skill at weaving. Her cloths were so beautiful that merchants travelled from distant towns to buy them. The patterns she created were unlike anything anyone had ever seen — swirling designs of deep blue and gold that seemed to shimmer in the sunlight. People said that Nadia's fingers moved as if guided by the wind itself.
Nadia was proud of her talent, and she guarded her methods closely. When younger women in the village asked her to teach them her techniques, she always refused. "These skills took me years to master," she would say, shaking her head firmly. "Why should I simply give them away? My knowledge is what makes my cloth valuable."
Her neighbour, an elderly man named Isaac, often watched her work from his doorstep. He had lived in the village all his life and was respected for his wisdom. "Nadia," he said one morning, stepping closer to her loom, "a river that does not share its water becomes a swamp. It grows stale and useless."
"I am no river, Uncle," Nadia replied with a polite but dismissive smile. "I am a well — and my water is for my family alone. I have worked hard to fill it, and I will not let others take from it."
Isaac shook his head sadly but said nothing more. He had learned long ago that people must discover certain truths for themselves.
One evening, dark clouds gathered on the horizon. The monsoon winds came earlier than anyone expected, howling through the village with tremendous force. Nadia rushed outside to protect her precious loom, but the wind was far too powerful. It tore through her workshop like a wild animal, snapping the wooden frame and scattering her finest silk threads across the muddy ground. The rain that followed soaked everything. By morning, her workshop was a ruin — her loom, her thread, her finished cloths, all destroyed.
Nadia sat among the wreckage, tears streaming down her face. She had no money to replace her loom, and the silk merchants would not return for many months. Without her weaving, she had no way to earn a living for her family. For the first time in her life, she felt completely helpless.

readings.title: Review text 2: Sports and Health: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 2: Sports and Health! In this unit, you will explore the exciting world of sports, fitness, and healthy living. You will read about how athletes train, what makes a healthy lifestyle, and how sports bring communities together across East Africa and beyond. Along the way, you will learn powerful new vocabulary, master an important grammar structure called the First Conditional, and develop your writing and speaking skills through creative projects like writing a biography and delivering sports commentary.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use and understand 28 new vocabulary words related to science, the environment, and health
Identify and use the First Conditional to talk about possible future events and their results
Use unless as an alternative to if not in conditional sentences
Read and understand an informative passage about the science behind sports and health
Write a short biography of a sportsperson using the third person
Write and deliver a piece of sports commentary using exciting and descriptive language
Distinguish between facts and opinions in texts about health and fitness
Practise speaking and listening skills through role-play, interviews, and presentations
Evaluate health habits and understand the importance of teamwork
Before you begin reading, take a moment to study the important words you will meet in this unit. These words come from your unit story and from the topics of science, health, and the environment. Knowing these words will help you understand the texts and complete the activities with confidence. Read through the table below, say each word aloud, and try to use it in a short sentence of your own.
Reading: Training Champions — The Science Behind Sports
Read the passage below carefully. Think about the main ideas, notice any new vocabulary, and pay attention to sentences that use the First Conditional structure (if + present simple, will + verb).
In many East African communities, sports have always been more than a game. From the famous long-distance runners of Kenya and Ethiopia to the basketball courts of Mogadishu and the football pitches of Nairobi, physical activity brings people together and keeps them strong. But have you ever wondered what happens inside your body when you exercise? The answer involves some fascinating biological processes that scientists are still studying today.
When you run, swim, or play football, your muscles need more energy. Your heart beats faster to pump blood around your body. This blood carries oxygen to your muscles so they can keep working. If you train regularly, your heart will become stronger and more efficient. It will pump more blood with each beat, which means it will not have to work as hard during rest. This is why athletes often have lower resting heart rates than people who do not exercise.

readings.title: Review text 3: Life in the Wild: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 3: Life in the Wild! In this unit, you will explore the natural world through the lens of East African ecosystems. You will discover how animals, plants, and their environments are connected in amazing ways. Every living thing depends on something else to survive, and understanding these connections helps us take better care of the world around us.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary.
Use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature.
Build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where.
Write an animal research report with clear paragraphs and accurate information.
Discuss how humans affect wildlife and what can be done to protect endangered species.
Identify and use key vocabulary related to the natural world: habitat, predator, prey, species, ecosystem, and more.
Read, understand, and respond to a text about wildlife and survival.
Speak confidently about the natural world and present your findings to others.
Before you begin reading, make sure you understand these important words. You will meet them again and again throughout the unit. Study the full definitions in your Vocabulary companion file.
ecosystem – a community of living things and their environment, all connected together
habitat – the natural home of an animal or plant
food chain – a series showing which creature eats which
predator – an animal that hunts other animals for food
prey – an animal that is hunted by another animal
species – a group of living things that are the same kind
amphibian – an animal that lives both in water and on land
carbon – a basic element found in all living things
observable – something that can be seen or noticed
crimson – a deep red colour
vicinity – the area around a particular place
endure – to keep going through something difficult without giving up
threat – something that can cause harm or danger
treacherous – dangerous and not safe, often not what it seems
starvation – the condition of not having enough food
Reading: The Web of Life in East Africa
Read the following passage carefully. As you read, pay attention to the present simple tense used to state facts, and notice the relative clauses that add extra information about animals and places. The words in bold are from your vocabulary list.
East Africa is a region which stretches from the dry plains of Somalia to the green highlands of Ethiopia and the vast savannahs of Kenya and Tanzania. It is home to one of the richest collections of wildlife on Earth. Every animal, plant, and insect plays a part in the great web of life. Scientists call this web an ecosystem — a community of living things that depend on each other and on the land, water, and air around them.

readings.title: Review text 4: Inventions and Creativity: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Work through the Grammar file on the Past Simple Tense (reviewed), including will/won’t and prepositional verbs.
Read the Story file, “The Last Straits”, and answer the reflection questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment at the end.
Before you read the passage, make sure you know these important words. You will meet them throughout this unit. Study them carefully, say each example sentence aloud, and try to use each word in a sentence of your own.
Reading: “Inventions That Changed Everyday Life”
Read this passage carefully. It describes how inventions have changed the way people live, work, and solve problems. Pay attention to the past simple tense — many sentences use it to describe events that already happened. As you read, underline any vocabulary words from this unit that you recognise.
For thousands of years, human beings have solved problems by inventing new tools and machines. Some inventions were simple, like the wheel or the needle, but they changed life forever. Others were complex, like the printing press or the electric generator, and they opened doors that nobody imagined before.
One of the most important inventions in history was the compass. Before the compass existed, sailors relied on the stars for navigation. If clouds covered the sky, ships lost their way. Chinese inventors created the first magnetic compass over a thousand years ago. Once sailors had this tool, they explored new territories, crossed dangerous straits, and connected civilizations that had never met before. The compass did not just change travel — it changed the way humans understood the whole world.
Another invention that transformed daily life was the printing press. In the year 1440, a German inventor named Ibrahim Gutenberg proposed a machine that could print books quickly. Before his invention, every book was copied by hand, which took months. The printing press made books cheaper and easier to produce. Within a few decades, millions of people who had never owned a book could finally read and learn. Knowledge was no longer something only the elite controlled — it belonged to everyone.
In the modern era, the electric generator changed how people lived and worked. A generator converts movement into electricity. Once communities had electricity, they could light their homes, power machines, and store food safely. In many parts of East Africa and the wider world, generators still provide power to schools, hospitals, and homes that are not connected to the main electricity grid. A single generator can make the difference between a community that struggles in the dark and one that thrives with light.

readings.title: Review text 5: Money Matters: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Speak clearly when presenting your opinion or discussing money habits.
Think critically about smart spending, the value of money, and the difference between needs and wants.
Understand and enjoy the story "The Notice Board" and explore themes of courage, fairness, and personal growth.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn the unit words a few at a time and say the example sentences aloud.
Work through the Grammar file. Focus especially on comparative adverbs, as they will appear throughout the unit.
Read the Story file, "The Notice Board", and answer the comprehension and discussion questions.
Work through the sections below: Key Vocabulary → Reading → Understanding the Text → Grammar Focus → Grammar Practice → Writing Workshop → Listening & Speaking → Check Your Learning.
Finish with the Check Your Learning self-assessment to see what you have achieved.
Here are some of the most important words you will meet in this unit. You will find the full word list with definitions and examples in your Vocabulary companion file. For now, read through these words and make sure you can say each one aloud.
The passage below is about money, saving, and making good financial choices. It is written for young people your age who are starting to think about how money works. As you read, think about these questions:
Money, Markets, and Making Good Choices
Every morning in cities and towns across East Africa, markets come alive. Stalls are set up with colourful fabrics, fresh fruit, and bags of rice and sugar. Shopkeepers call out prices. Customers count their coins carefully. A woman buys enough flour for the week. A boy saves his last few shillings instead of buying sweets. A grandmother bargains firmly for a fair price on vegetables. Money moves from hand to hand, and each decision — big or small — tells a story about how people think about value.
But what is money, really? Money is a tool. It is not good or bad on its own. What matters is how we use it. Financial literacy means understanding how money works and learning to make smart choices with it. This includes knowing the difference between needs and wants. A need is something you must have to live safely and healthily, like food, clean water, shelter, and clothing. A want is something nice to have but not essential, like a new toy, a fancy phone case, or extra sweets after school.
One of the most crucial money skills is saving. Saving means putting aside part of your money for the future instead of spending it all at once. Even small amounts add up over time. If you save just a few coins each week, after several months you might have enough for something substantial — a book you have wanted, a gift for someone you love, or a contribution to a family goal. In Islam, we are taught to be balanced with money: not to waste it, not to hoard it selfishly, and to always share with those who have less. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, said that the best of people are those who are most beneficial to others. Giving sadaqah is not just generous — it is a way of purifying our wealth and strengthening our community.

grammar.title: Sentence accuracy audit
grammar.explanation: Review agreement, tense and complete sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Every sentence needs a clear subject and verb.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Correct six sentences from your portfolio.

grammar.title: Tense control
grammar.explanation: Check that past, present and future time are clear.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Yesterday I visited. Today I learn. Tomorrow I will present.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Revise six verbs and explain each tense choice.

grammar.title: Questions and responses
grammar.explanation: Use accurate Wh- and yes/no questions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Where did you learn this? Why is it important?
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.
grammar.practice: Write and answer six audience questions.

grammar.title: Description and precision
grammar.explanation: Improve nouns, verbs, adjectives and adverbs.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: The thoughtful learner explained the idea clearly.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Joining ideas
grammar.explanation: Connect ideas with and, but, because, so and when.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: I revised my story because feedback helped me.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

grammar.title: Final editing conference
grammar.explanation: Apply capitals, spelling, punctuation and paragraphing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read, mark, revise, then read again.
grammar.commonMistake: Do not change language randomly; explain how the revision improves meaning or accuracy.

writing.title: Portfolio evidence captions
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a precise paragraph about each of three selected pieces and explain what each demonstrates.
writing.modelText: This revised report shows that I can organize evidence and support an evaluation.
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Research proposal
writing.promptAndInstructions: Explain your question, audience, approved sources, product and plan in three organized paragraphs.
writing.modelText: My project will investigate how communities prepare for natural hazards using two approved sources.
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Researched first draft
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write the complete first draft of your capstone product with clear sections and source evidence.
writing.modelText: Use a clear introduction, organized sections and paragraphs, synthesized evidence, and a conclusion suited to your audience.
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Revision from feedback
writing.promptAndInstructions: Record three feedback points and rewrite the sections they improve.
writing.modelText: Feedback asked me to compare evidence, so I connected details from Units 3 and 7 and explained their significance.
writing.sentenceStarter: One substantial improvement I made was...
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Presentation notes
writing.promptAndInstructions: Prepare concise cue cards for a four-minute presentation and question defence.
writing.modelText: Opening; research question; claims; evidence; viewpoint; audience questions; closing.
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

writing.title: Grade 6 reflection
writing.promptAndInstructions: Evaluate your strongest progress, one challenge, and a specific Grade 7 goal.
writing.modelText: I became a stronger reader because I learned to synthesize and evaluate evidence. Next, I will improve source comparison.
writing.expectedLength: Three to five paragraphs with clear structure
writing.successCriteria: I addressed my audience; organised connected sections and paragraphs; used precise vocabulary; synthesized relevant evidence; considered viewpoint; checked grammar and punctuation; revised from feedback
writing.support: Use a research organizer, planning frame, word bank and approved source models.
writing.extension: Evaluate source choices, vary sentence structures, strengthen transitions and write a defensible conclusion.

quizzes.question: What does 'illegal' mean?
quizzes.options: A tool or device used to make music or do a specific job | Gave or provided something that was needed | Not allowed by law or rules | The act of going into a place, or the way to get in
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not allowed by law or rules
quizzes.explanation: It is illegal to steal someone else's property.

quizzes.question: What does 'Christian' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who follows the teachings of Jesus Christ | A pleasing combination of things that work well together; peace | A tool or device used to make music or do a specific job | Gave or provided something that was needed
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who follows the teachings of Jesus Christ
quizzes.explanation: My friend is a Christian and goes to church every Sunday.

quizzes.question: What does 'geographic' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who speaks for God or tells what will happen in the future | Related to the physical features of the Earth and how places are arranged | The ability to create ideas or pictures in your mind | The ability to make choices and take action
quizzes.correctAnswer: Related to the physical features of the Earth and how places are arranged
quizzes.explanation: The geographic features of our country include mountains, rivers, and valleys.

quizzes.question: What does 'originally' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who speaks for God or tells what will happen in the future | At the beginning or in the first place | The ability to make choices and take action | To become worse or to decline in quality
quizzes.correctAnswer: At the beginning or in the first place
quizzes.explanation: Originally, the story was written as a book before it became a movie.

quizzes.question: What does 'threat' mean?
quizzes.options: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. | A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others. | Something that can cause harm or danger. | To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something that can cause harm or danger.
quizzes.explanation: The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.

quizzes.question: What does 'treacherous' mean?
quizzes.options: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. | Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems. | To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity. | To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems.
quizzes.explanation: The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.

quizzes.question: What does 'straits' mean?
quizzes.options: A group of people travelling together, often across a desert. | A narrow body of water between two pieces of land. | A short trip or journey, usually for fun or learning. | An area of land that belongs to a person, group, or country.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A narrow body of water between two pieces of land.
quizzes.explanation: The ships passed through the straits to get to the other side of the island.

quizzes.question: What does 'explorer' mean?
quizzes.options: A group of people travelling together, often across a desert. | A person who travels to new places to discover things. | An area of land that belongs to a person, group, or country. | The process of finding your way from one place to another.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who travels to new places to discover things.
quizzes.explanation: The explorer found a new island in the ocean.

quizzes.question: What does 'humble' mean?
quizzes.options: Easily affected by things around you, like feelings or physical things. | In a strong and steady way; not easily moved or changed. | Not enough; very small in amount or quality. | Not thinking that you are better than others; modest.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not thinking that you are better than others; modest.
quizzes.explanation: Even though he won the race, he remained humble and thanked his friends for their support.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Review text 4: Inventions and Creativity: source text 2': Read the Story file, “The Last _____”, and answer the reflection questions.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Read the Story file, “The Last Straits”, and answer the reflection questions.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages
